candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made into a rigorous theorem showing that durable, redundant, temporally consistent record formation scales uniquely with squared amplitude rather than some other function of amplitude? The central unresolved tension is that the argument currently gestures at copying, overlap, norm conservation, and decoherence, but does not yet derive a unique measure from explicit dynamics.

How can 'record durability' be defined non-circularly and independently of already assuming Born-weighted typicality? There is an open risk that notions like redundancy, recoverability, coherence of histories, or 'dominating the space of stable observational worlds' may smuggle in the very probabilistic weighting the conjecture aims to explain.

Do low-amplitude branches actually have reduced capacity to host stable observers and consistent memories under unitary evolution, or can they be internally just as coherent as high-amplitude branches despite lower measure? This is a major open question because standard Everettian worries suggest amplitude affects weighting, not local branch quality, so the conjecture needs to explain why anomalous-frequency branches would be record-fragile rather than merely atypical.
